review: Many great riffs and grooves, but inconsistent songwriting.
Art-sy, prog-sy folk-rock. Rich instrumentation and richer choir-like vocal harmonies.

record: Wait Long by the River and the Bodies of your Ennemies Will Float by

review: Double the drummers, double the bassists, double the (screaming) singers, and a guitarist too busy making noise to play riffs. Awesome.
New wavey Joy Division-ish post-punk. Punky guitars, mandatory synths (it's 1980).
Sharp guitars, noisy mix, quirky vocals. Someone listened to Black Midi, and probably Primus too.
Classic. Tight grooves and sharp, rythmic guitar playing.
Some people say Whatever People Say I Am is better. I disagree.
Adding a saxophone and fusing-in more free jazz and dub elements, this is Black Eyes at their noisiest and most experimental.

Post-rock/midwest emo Neutral Milk Hotel. Layered guitars and a crippling addiction to fade-outs. Crazy to have so many multiple-parts tracks yet no proper ending for most of them, with a lot of them overstaying their welcome.
Classic Fugazi: interesting dynamics, tight bass and drums grooves, harmonized riffage and great vocals.

Listened to this only once before, and felt unimpressed, but it's actually better than I remembered. Quite a different vibe to other contemporaries, more 'urban' than 'artsy'. Tracks are more linear and lowkey, with dance/electronic influences which are not that common in this scene.
Alternates between long multi-part tracks, shorter folk-rocks ballads. Strong 60s influences (there's even a mellotron!).
Cabaret post-punk, somehow? Theatrical and jazzy.
More on the indie/pop side of the current post-punk wave. Some potential but something just doesn't click for me, probably a bit too pop.
